tier_2_handbook.definition: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) is a poorly differentiated, high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma originating from neuroendocrine cells of the lung. It is defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) tumor classification system as a malignant epithelial tumor with distinctive histologic features: small blue cells with scant cytoplasm, high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, finely granular chromatin, and absent or inconspicuous nucleoli [23,36]. SCLC is characterized by rapid doubling time, high growth fraction, and early widespread metastases. The American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM staging (8th edition) is used in conjunction with the Veterans Administration two-stage classification. Limited-stage SCLC is defined as stage I–III (T any, N any, M0) that can be safely encompassed within a tolerable radiation field, excluding T3–4 due to multiple extensive lung nodules or excessive tumor/nodal volume. Extensive-stage SCLC is defined as stage IV (T any, N any, M1a/b/c) or T3–4 with disease too extensive for definitive radiation [17,70]. Anatomically, SCLC arises in the lung but may also present as combined SCLC with non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) histology, or transform from NSCLC during treatment [43,56,61-64]. Extrapulmonary small cell carcinomas are rare but share similar biologic behavior [6,7]. The diagnosis requires histologic or cytologic confirmation, often supported by immunohistochemical markers of neuroendocrine differentiation including INSM1, CD56, synaptophysin, and chromogranin A, with TTF-1 positive in 85%–90% of cases [11,12,15-18]. Mitotic count is essential for grading: SCLC shows >10 mitoses per 2 mm², distinguishing it from typical (0–1 mitoses/2 mm²) and atypical (2–10 mitoses/2 mm²) carcinoids [1,2]. Ki-67 proliferative index is typically 50%–100% [1]. In limited samples, immunohistochemistry for RB1 and p53 aids differentiation: carcinoid tumors generally retain RB1 expression and show wild-type p53 staining, while most high-grade neuroendocrine carcinomas demonstrate loss of RB1 and/or aberrant p53 expression (diffuse strong nuclear staining or complete absence) [1]. Combined SCLC consists of SCLC admixed with any NSCLC subtype (squamous, adenocarcinoma, spindle/pleomorphic, or large cell carcinoma); for large-cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC), at least 10% LCNEC morphology is required [1].

subtypes.name: Pure SCLC
subtypes.description: Classic small cell lung carcinoma with no detectable non-small cell component. Confirmed by histology showing small blue cells with scant cytoplasm, high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, granular chromatin, and absent nucleoli. Nearly all cases express neuroendocrine markers (INSM1, synaptophysin, chromogranin A, CD56). Ki-67 proliferative index is 50%–100%. Mitotic count exceeds 10 per 2 mm².
subtypes.key_associations: Strongly associated with cigarette smoking; nearly 95% of cases occur in smokers. High sensitivity to initial chemotherapy and radiotherapy but frequent relapse. Characterized by RB1 loss (90%) and TP53 mutations (90%) [55,56].

subtypes.name: Combined SCLC
subtypes.description: SCLC admixed with any non-small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC) histology, including squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, spindle/pleomorphic carcinoma, or large cell carcinoma. No minimal percentage of NSCLC component is required; the presence of any NSCLC element qualifies as combined SCLC, except for LCNEC where at least 10% LCNEC morphology is needed [1,43,56,61-64].
subtypes.frequency: More frequent in limited-stage SCLC; exact prevalence varies but estimated at 10–20% of SCLC cases.
subtypes.key_associations: Higher proportion of limited-stage disease at diagnosis. Treated similarly to pure SCLC due to the more aggressive SCLC component. May arise from clonal evolution after therapy [43,56,65].

subtypes.name: Transformed SCLC from NSCLC
subtypes.description: A rare acquired histologic transformation from NSCLC (most commonly EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma) to SCLC, typically after treatment with tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) or immune checkpoint inhibitors. The transformed cells exhibit classic SCLC morphology and neuroendocrine markers. This entity is recognized as a resistance mechanism [67,68].
subtypes.frequency: Rare, estimated 3–14% of EGFR-mutant NSCLC after TKI therapy.
subtypes.key_associations: Often retains the original oncogenic driver (e.g., EGFR mutation). Treatment follows SCLC guidelines, but immunotherapy is avoided if TKIs are continued due to known toxicity [45,47,48]. Referral to expert centers recommended.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: SCLC is driven by near-universal inactivation of tumor suppressor genes TP53 and RB1. Genomic studies demonstrate TP53 mutations in >90% and RB1 loss (combined mutation, deletion, or loss of expression) in >90% of SCLC cases [55,56]. This dual inactivation is considered a defining molecular feature, leading to unchecked cell cycle progression and genomic instability. Additional recurrent alterations include amplification of MYC family members (MYC, MYCL, MYCN) in ~20% of cases, and loss-of-function mutations in NOTCH family genes (e.g., NOTCH1, NOTCH2, NOTCH3), which contribute to neuroendocrine differentiation [55,56]. The delta-like ligand 3 (DLL3), a Notch inhibitory ligand, is overexpressed on the surface of 85–94% of SCLC cells, providing a therapeutic target for bispecific T-cell engagers [195]. Other pathways implicated include PTEN loss, FGFR1 amplification, and alterations in the CREBBP/EP300 chromatin remodeling complex. SCLC exhibits high tumor mutational burden, typically >10 mutations per megabase, largely attributable to tobacco carcinogen exposure [55-57]. Molecular subclassification based on transcription factor expression has been proposed: ASCL1 (SCLC-A), NEUROD1 (SCLC-N), POU2F3 (SCLC-P), and YAP1 (SCLC-Y). These subtypes correlate with neuroendocrine differentiation and potential therapeutic vulnerabilities, though not yet used for routine clinical decision-making. POU2F3 immunohistochemistry can be used to identify neuroendocrine-low/negative SCLC [13,14]. Additionally, RB1 and p53 immunohistochemistry patterns help distinguish SCLC from carcinoid tumors: carcinoids retain RB1 and show wild-type p53 pattern, while SCLC shows loss of RB1 and aberrant p53 (overexpression or null) [1]. Methylation of the MGMT promoter has been reported as a potential biomarker for temozolomide response in a subset of relapsed patients [234].
tier_2_handbook.epidemiology: In 2026, SCLC is estimated to account for approximately 13% of all lung cancer diagnoses, with an estimated 30,000 new cases in the United States [1]. The incidence of SCLC has been declining over recent decades, largely attributed to decreased smoking prevalence. However, the frequency among females is increasing, and the male-to-female incidence ratio has approached 1:1 in the last few years [2]. Nearly all cases (≥95%) are attributable to cigarette smoking [3]. The median age at diagnosis is >70 years; incidence rises with age [171]. Approximately two-thirds of patients present with extensive-stage disease (hematogenous metastases), while one-third have limited-stage disease confined to the chest [16]. SCLC is more common in African American men compared to other racial/ethnic groups. Globally, SCLC incidence mirrors smoking patterns, with higher rates in regions with high tobacco consumption. Long-term survival remains poor; for extensive-stage SCLC, median overall survival with modern chemoimmunotherapy is approximately 12–13 months, with 2-year survival rates around 20–25% [118-120]. For limited-stage SCLC, concurrent chemoradiation yields median survival of 25–30 months and 5-year survival of 31–34% [105]. Despite initial chemosensitivity, most patients eventually relapse and die of recurrent disease. Second primary lung cancers (both SCLC and NSCLC) occur in long-term survivors, emphasizing the need for continued surveillance and smoking cessation [190,191].

risk_factors.detail: Nearly all cases of SCLC are attributable to cigarette smoking, with an attributable risk of approximately 95% [3]. Active smoking during treatment is associated with increased toxicity and shorter survival [4]. Secondhand smoke exposure may also contribute but is less well quantified.
risk_factors.strength: Strongest known risk factor; relative risk for SCLC in smokers vs. never-smokers exceeds 20-fold. Dose-response relationship exists with duration and intensity of smoking.

risk_factors.detail: Incidence increases with age; median age at diagnosis is >70 years [171]. Older age is also a risk factor for increased toxicity from therapy.
risk_factors.strength: Established risk factor; incidence rises steeply after age 50.

risk_factors.detail: Historically higher incidence in males, but male-to-female ratio has converged to near 1:1 in recent years [2].
Exposure to ionizing radiation to the chest (e.g., for Hodgkin lymphoma or breast cancer) increases risk of developing SCLC as a second primary.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate risk increase; absolute risk low.

risk_factors.detail: Exposure to asbestos, arsenic, chromium, nickel, and other carcinogens may increase SCLC risk, particularly in combination with smoking.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate, but smoking is the dominant risk factor.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Patients with small cell lung cancer (SCLC) typically present with a large hilar mass and bulky mediastinal lymphadenopathy that cause cough and dyspnea. Frequently, patients present with symptoms of widespread metastatic disease, such as weight loss, debility, bone pain, and neurologic compromise. It is uncommon for patients to present with a solitary peripheral nodule without central adenopathy; in this situation, fine-needle aspiration may not adequately differentiate SCLC from low-grade or intermediate-grade neuroendocrine tumors. Many neurologic and endocrine paraneoplastic syndromes, including Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS), encephalomyelitis, sensory neuropathy, SIADH, and Cushing syndrome, are associated with SCLC. The classic histology on hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) includes small blue cells with scant cytoplasm, high nuclear/cytoplasmic ratio, fine granular chromatin, and absent or inconspicuous nucleoli. SCLC is characterized by a rapid doubling time, high growth fraction, and early development of widespread metastases; approximately one third of patients present with limited disease confined to the chest, while most present with hematogenous metastases. Nearly all cases are attributable to cigarette smoking, and the incidence in females is increasing, with the male-to-female ratio approaching 1:1.

symptoms.name: Cough
symptoms.detail: Caused by endobronchial irritation or bronchial compression from a central mass.

symptoms.name: Hemoptysis
symptoms.detail: Usually from a central or cavitary lesion.

symptoms.name: Wheezing
symptoms.detail: Due to a partially obstructing endobronchial lesion.

symptoms.name: Fever
symptoms.detail: May indicate postobstructive pneumonia.

symptoms.name: Dyspnea
symptoms.detail: From bronchial obstruction, pneumonia, or pleural effusion.

symptoms.name: Hoarseness
symptoms.detail: Left vocal cord paralysis due to tumor invasion or lymphadenopathy in the aortopulmonary window.

symptoms.name: Hemidiaphragm elevation
symptoms.detail: Due to phrenic nerve compression.

symptoms.name: Dysphagia
symptoms.detail: Due to esophageal compression from mediastinal disease.

symptoms.name: Chest pain
symptoms.detail: Involvement of pleura or chest wall; often dull and non-localized.

symptoms.name: Superior vena cava syndrome
symptoms.detail: Due to local invasion into mediastinum or lymphadenopathy in right paratracheal region.

symptoms.name: Pericardial effusion and tamponade
symptoms.detail: Cardiac involvement can cause effusion and hemodynamic compromise.

symptoms.name: Cervical or supraclavicular lymph node enlargement
symptoms.detail: Palpable nodes indicating regional lymphatic spread.

symptoms.name: Headache (brain metastases)
symptoms.detail: Often the first symptom of brain involvement; may be accompanied by focal weakness, numbness, confusion, slurred speech, gait instability, or incoordination.

symptoms.name: Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis
symptoms.detail: Headache, confusion, cranial nerve palsy, diplopia, slurred speech, radicular back pain, spinal cord compression.

symptoms.name: Adrenal metastases
symptoms.detail: Mid-back or flank pain, costovertebral angle tenderness; rarely adrenal insufficiency.

symptoms.name: Liver metastases
symptoms.detail: Right upper quadrant pain or tenderness, jaundice, fatigue, fever, hepatomegaly.

symptoms.name: Bone metastases
symptoms.detail: Bone pain; spinal cord compression may cause back pain, muscle weakness, numbness, paresthesia, loss of bowel and bladder control.

symptoms.name: Constitutional symptoms
symptoms.detail: Anorexia, cachexia (weight loss), fatigue.

symptoms.name: SIADH (hyponatremia)
symptoms.detail: Ectopic vasopressin secretion; clinically significant hyponatremia in 5–10% of SCLC patients. Symptoms: malaise, weakness, confusion, obtundation, volume depletion, nausea.

symptoms.name: Cushing syndrome
symptoms.detail: Ectopic ACTH secretion. Symptoms: weight gain, moon facies, hypertension, hyperglycemia, generalized weakness.

symptoms.name: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome
symptoms.detail: Proximal muscle weakness, autonomic dysfunction; often precedes diagnosis. Neurologic workup includes PQ- and N-type VGCC antibodies.

symptoms.name: Subacute cerebellar degeneration
symptoms.detail: Ataxia, dysarthria.

symptoms.name: Encephalomyelitis
symptoms.detail: Confusion, obtundation, dementia.

symptoms.name: Sensory neuropathy
symptoms.detail: Pain, sensory loss.

symptoms.name: Cancer-associated retinopathy
symptoms.detail: Visual loss, photosensitivity.

symptoms.name: Anemia
symptoms.detail: May occur as a paraneoplastic hematologic manifestation.

symptoms.name: Leukemoid reaction
symptoms.detail: Leukocytosis.

symptoms.name: Trousseau syndrome
symptoms.detail: Migratory thrombophlebitis.

signs.name: Vocal cord paralysis (hoarseness)
signs.detail: Left vocal cord paralysis due to tumor or lymphadenopathy in aortopulmonary window.

signs.name: Hemidiaphragm elevation
signs.detail: Phrenic nerve compression causing elevated hemidiaphragm on imaging.

signs.name: Superior vena cava syndrome
signs.detail: Facial swelling, distended neck veins, upper extremity edema.

signs.name: Bone tenderness
signs.detail: Focal pain on palpation over metastatic bone lesions.

signs.name: Neurologic deficits
signs.detail: Focal weakness, sensory loss, ataxia, or cranial nerve palsies from brain or leptomeningeal metastases.

signs.name: Cushingoid features
signs.detail: Moon facies, central obesity, hypertension, hyperglycemia from ectopic ACTH.

atypical_or_missed: Presentation as a solitary peripheral nodule without central adenopathy, leading to misdiagnosis as carcinoid or other neuroendocrine tumor – especially in non-smokers.
Paraneoplastic syndromes (e.g., LEMS, encephalomyelitis, SIADH) appearing before detection of the lung tumor, delaying cancer diagnosis.
Combined SCLC with NSCLC histology, which may be underrecognized and requires careful pathologic review.
Brain metastases as the first manifestation without significant pulmonary symptoms.
Transformation of NSCLC to SCLC after treatment with EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors or immune checkpoint inhibitors.

red_flags: Hemoptysis – requires urgent bronchoscopy and imaging to identify central lesion.
Superior vena cava syndrome – urgent imaging and intervention to relieve obstruction.
Spinal cord compression symptoms (back pain, muscle weakness, numbness, loss of bowel/bladder control) – requires immediate steroids and MRI, followed by RT or surgical stabilization.
Symptomatic brain metastases (headache, focal weakness, confusion, seizures, gait instability) – requires urgent brain MRI and management with steroids and RT.
Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis (headache, cranial nerve palsies, radicular pain) – urgent CSF analysis and brain/spine MRI.
Clinically significant hyponatremia from SIADH (symptomatic or severe) – requires fluid restriction, saline infusion, or vasopressin receptor inhibitors.
Rapid decline in performance status or new neurologic symptoms during or after treatment – may indicate progressive disease or paraneoplastic syndrome.
Development of a new pulmonary nodule during surveillance – requires workup for potential new primary lung cancer.

diagnostic.rationale: Assess symptoms, performance status, smoking history, and signs of metastatic disease.
diagnostic.key_findings: Cough, dyspnea, hemoptysis, weight loss, neurologic deficits, lymphadenopathy.

diagnostic.rationale: Confirm SCLC diagnosis and distinguish from other neuroendocrine tumors.
diagnostic.key_findings: Small blue cells with scant cytoplasm, high nuclear/cytoplasmic ratio, fine granular chromatin, absent nucleoli. Mitotic count >10 per 2 mm².

diagnostic.rationale: Confirm neuroendocrine differentiation and rule out NSCLC and carcinoid.
diagnostic.key_findings: Positive for cytokeratin (AE1/AE3, CAM5.2), neuroendocrine markers (INSM1, CD56, synaptophysin, chromogranin A), TTF-1 (85–90%). Ki-67 typically 50–100%. RB1 loss and/or aberrant p53 expression in high-grade carcinomas.

diagnostic.rationale: Detect anemia, thrombocytopenia, or leukocytosis from bone marrow involvement or paraneoplasia.
diagnostic.key_findings: Neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, nucleated RBCs may indicate bone marrow infiltration.

diagnostic.test: Electrolytes, liver function tests (LFTs), blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine
diagnostic.rationale: Assess baseline organ function and detect hyponatremia (SIADH) or liver metastases.

diagnostic.test: CT scan of chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Evaluate primary tumor, mediastinal lymph nodes, and distant metastases to liver, adrenals, and other sites.
diagnostic.key_findings: Large hilar mass, bulky mediastinal lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion, liver/adrenal nodules.

diagnostic.test: Brain MRI with contrast (preferred) or CT with contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Detect brain metastases; more sensitive than CT.
Clarify extent of disease, detect extrathoracic metastases, and guide RT planning.
diagnostic.key_findings: Hypermetabolic foci in primary tumor, lymph nodes, and distant sites. May upstage or downstage disease.

diagnostic.rationale: Continued smoking reduces treatment efficacy and increases toxicity and second primary cancers.
diagnostic.key_findings: Use the 5 A’s Framework; refer to NCCN Guidelines for Smoking Cessation.

diagnostic.rationale: Early discussion of goals of care and symptom management.
diagnostic.key_findings: Improves quality of life and may impact treatment planning.

diagnostic.test: Consider biomarker testing (blood, tissue, or both)
diagnostic.rationale: May identify actionable alterations in rare cases (never/light smokers, remote smoking history, diagnostic dilemma, or relapse).
diagnostic.key_findings: Genomic profiling can change management in select patients.

staging.rationale: If pleural effusion is present, rule out malignant involvement (indicates extensive stage).
staging.indication: Pleural effusion large enough for safe access.

staging.rationale: Assess candidacy for surgery or definitive RT.
staging.indication: During evaluation for surgery or definitive radiation therapy.

staging.rationale: Evaluate bone metastases if FDG-PET/CT is equivocal or not available.
staging.indication: FDG-PET/CT equivocal for bone lesions, or bone scan if PET unavailable.

staging.rationale: Detect bone marrow involvement in select patients with unexplained cytopenias.
staging.indication: Select patients with nucleated RBCs on peripheral smear, neutropenia, or thrombocytopenia suggestive of marrow infiltration; only if it changes clinical management.

staging.test: Pathologic lymph node staging (EBUS-TBNA, mediastinoscopy, mediastinotomy, EUS, VATS)
staging.rationale: Confirm nodal status before surgery or to guide RT fields.
staging.indication: Clinical stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0) for surgical candidates; consider for stage IIB–IIIC to inform RT, especially if cN0.

staging.rationale: Review of all staging data to determine resectability and best treatment sequence.

biomarkers.test: Immunohistochemistry for neuroendocrine markers (INSM1, CD56, synaptophysin, chromogranin A)
biomarkers.rationale: Confirm neuroendocrine differentiation; INSM1 has superior performance.
biomarkers.when: On all biopsy or cytology samples when morphology is suspicious for SCLC.

biomarkers.rationale: Positive in 85–90% of SCLC, helps distinguish from other small cell carcinomas.
Essential for distinguishing SCLC (50–100%) from carcinoid tumors (<20%) when mitotic counting is difficult.
biomarkers.when: Small biopsies with crushed or necrotic cells.

biomarkers.rationale: Loss of RB1 and aberrant p53 staining (diffuse strong or null pattern) differentiate high-grade NEC from carcinoid.
biomarkers.when: Cases with diagnostic difficulty between SCLC and atypical carcinoid.

biomarkers.rationale: Consider for cases with suspicious SCLC morphology but negative for standard neuroendocrine markers.
biomarkers.when: Suspicious morphology without expression of INSM1, CD56, synaptophysin, chromogranin A.

biomarkers.rationale: Distinguish SCLC from poorly differentiated NSCLC and combined carcinoma. Note: p40/p63 can be focally positive in SCLC.
biomarkers.when: When differential includes NSCLC or combined SCLC.

biomarkers.test: Biomarker testing (comprehensive molecular profiling via blood, tissue, or both)
biomarkers.rationale: May detect actionable alterations (e.g., oncogenic drivers) that could change management.
biomarkers.when: Rare cases: extensive-stage/relapsed SCLC patients who never smoked, lightly smoked, have remote smoking history, diagnostic/therapeutic dilemma, or at time of relapse if not previously done.

do_not_miss: Brain MRI with contrast (or CT if MRI contraindicated) for all patients at diagnosis and during surveillance.
FDG-PET/CT when needed to clarify disease extent and for RT planning (preferably within 4 weeks and no more than 8 weeks before treatment).
Pathologic confirmation of isolated or equivocal lesions on FDG-PET/CT if their involvement would change clinical management.
Lymph node staging (endobronchial ultrasound-guided biopsy preferred) before surgical resection in stage I–IIA SCLC.
Consideration of biomarker testing in never/light smokers or those with diagnostic dilemma.
Smoking cessation intervention at every visit.
avoid: Repeating scans to assess response during adjuvant or initial treatment in the absence of new symptoms (limited-stage SCLC).
FDG-PET/CT for routine follow-up; use only if contrast CT or MRI is contraindicated.
Bevacizumab is not recommended for treatment of SCLC.
Routine use of granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF) or granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) during concurrent chemoradiation (category 1 for not using GM-CSF).
PCI in patients with poor performance status (PS 3–4) or impaired neurocognitive function.
Pneumonectomy if required to encompass nodal metastatic disease; chemoradiation is preferred.

staging.system: American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) 8th edition TNM classification, 2017, combined with Veterans Administration (VA) two-stage system (limited-stage and extensive-stage).

t_stages.description: Primary tumor cannot be assessed, or tumor proven by the presence of malignant cells in sputum or bronchial washings but not visualized by imaging or bronchoscopy.
Carcinoma in situ: squamous cell carcinoma in situ (SCIS) or adenocarcinoma in situ (AIS) with pure lepidic pattern, ≤3 cm in greatest dimension.

Tumor ≤3 cm in greatest dimension, surrounded by lung or visceral pleura, without bronchoscopic evidence of invasion more proximal than the lobar bronchus (i.e., not in the main bronchus). Includes T1mi (minimally invasive adenocarcinoma), T1a (≤1 cm), T1b (>1 cm but ≤2 cm), T1c (>2 cm but ≤3 cm). A superficial, spreading tumor of any size whose invasive component is limited to the bronchial wall and may extend proximal to the main bronchus is classified as T1a.

Tumor >3 cm but ≤5 cm or having any of the following features: involves the main bronchus regardless of distance to carina but without carina involvement; invades visceral pleura (PL1 or PL2); associated with atelectasis or obstructive pneumonitis extending to hilar region involving part or all of the lung. T2a: >3 cm but ≤4 cm; T2b: >4 cm but ≤5 cm.

Tumor >5 cm but ≤7 cm in greatest dimension, or directly invading parietal pleura (PL3), chest wall (including superior sulcus tumors), phrenic nerve, parietal pericardium; or separate tumor nodule(s) in the same lobe as the primary.

Tumor >7 cm, or tumor of any size invading diaphragm, mediastinum, heart, great vessels, trachea, recurrent laryngeal nerve, esophagus, vertebral body, or carina; or separate tumor nodule(s) in an ipsilateral lobe different from that of the primary.

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
Metastasis in ipsilateral peribronchial and/or ipsilateral hilar lymph nodes and intrapulmonary nodes, including involvement by direct extension.
Metastasis in ipsilateral mediastinal and/or subcarinal lymph node(s).
Metastasis in contralateral mediastinal, contralateral hilar, ipsilateral or contralateral scalene, or supraclavicular lymph node(s).

m_stages.description: Distant metastasis cannot be assessed (not used in AJCC 8th edition, listed for completeness).
Separate tumor nodule(s) in a contralateral lobe; tumor with pleural or pericardial nodules or malignant pleural or pericardial effusion.
Single extrathoracic metastasis in a single organ (including involvement of a single nonregional node).
Multiple extrathoracic metastases in a single organ or in multiple organs.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor cells found in sputum or washings but not visualized.
Minimally invasive or ≤1 cm tumor, no nodal involvement.
Tumor >1 cm to 2 cm, no nodal involvement.
Tumor >2 cm to 3 cm, no nodal involvement.
Tumor >3 cm to 4 cm or with visceral pleura involvement without nodal disease.
Tumor >4 cm to 5 cm, no nodal involvement.

stage_groups.criteria: T1a–T2b with N1, or T3 N0 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor up to 5 cm with ipsilateral hilar node involvement, or T3 >5 to 7 cm without nodal disease.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (chemoradiation or surgery for selected T1-2 N1)

stage_groups.criteria: T1a–T2b with N2, or T3–T4 with N0–N1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Ipsilateral mediastinal node involvement or larger primary with limited nodal spread.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (chemoradiation; potential surgery for T3 N1 N0)

stage_groups.criteria: T1a–T2b with N3, or T3–T4 with N2
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Contralateral/supraclavicular node involvement or extensive mediastinal disease.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (chemoradiation if can be safely encompassed; otherwise extensive-stage)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locally advanced with contralateral or supraclavicular nodes.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (chemoradiation if tolerable; may be extensive-stage if too extensive)

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, Any N, M1a or M1b
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Contralateral lung nodule or pleural/pericardial involvement, or single extrathoracic metastasis.
Multiple extrathoracic metastases in one or more organs.

staging_pearls: Limited-stage SCLC is defined as stage I–III (T any, N any, M0) that can be safely treated with definitive radiation doses. Excludes T3–4 due to multiple lung nodules that are too extensive or have tumor/nodal volume too large to be encompassed in a tolerable radiation plan.
Extensive-stage SCLC is defined as stage IV (T any, N any, M1a/b/c) or T3–4 due to multiple extensive lung nodules or tumor/nodal volume too large for tolerable RT.

The VA two-stage system (limited vs extensive) remains widely used for clinical decision-making, but TNM staging is essential for selecting patients with T1–2,N0 disease eligible for surgery and for precise prognostication.

Contralateral mediastinal and ipsilateral supraclavicular lymphadenopathy are generally classified as limited-stage; contralateral hilar and supraclavicular involvement is more controversial and treatment is individualized.
Pathologic lymph node staging (endobronchial ultrasound, mediastinoscopy) is recommended before surgical resection to confirm N0 status.

Most pleural effusions in lung cancer are due to tumor; however, if multiple negative cytologies, fluid is nonbloody and not exudate, and clinical judgment dictates effusion is not tumor-related, it should be excluded as a staging element. Pericardial effusion uses same criteria.
The AJCC 8th edition staging tables used in this guideline are from 2017; the Panel adopted a combined approach using both TNM and VA classification.

The 5-year survival for each stage group is not specified in the guideline; however, for limited-stage SCLC treated with chemoradiation, 5-year overall survival is reported as 26–34% in historical trials.
The benefit of PCI is unclear in patients with pathologic stage I (T1–2a,N0,M0) SCLC after definitive surgery; these patients have a lower risk of brain metastases.

management_principles.overview: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) is a high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma characterized by rapid doubling time, high growth fraction, and early widespread metastases. Approximately 13% of lung tumors are SCLC, with an estimated 30,000 new cases in the US in 2026 [1]. Nearly all cases are attributable to cigarette smoking; smoking cessation counseling and intervention are strongly promoted [2]. Treatment philosophy is multimodal and stage-dependent: for limited-stage SCLC (LS-SCLC, stage I–III that can be safely treated with definitive radiation doses), the goal is cure using chemotherapy plus thoracic radiation therapy (RT), with surgery reserved for a small subset of clinical stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0) patients [3,4]. For extensive-stage SCLC (ES-SCLC, stage IV or T3–4 due to multiple extensive lung nodules), therapy is palliative, aiming to prolong survival and control symptoms, although long-term survival is rare [5]. Systemic therapy is essential at all stages, and RT has a potential role in definitive or palliative settings. Early integration of palliative care is recommended [6]. Workup should be expedited with studies performed in parallel whenever possible [7].

by_intent.patient_group: Limited-stage SCLC (stage I–III, safely treatable with definitive RT)
by_intent.approach: Chemotherapy plus concurrent thoracic RT (category 1 for PS 0–2) [8,9]; for clinical stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0), consider surgical resection (lobectomy with mediastinal lymph node dissection) followed by adjuvant systemic therapy, with or without mediastinal RT [10,11]. For medically inoperable patients, stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR) followed by systemic therapy is an option [12,13]. Consolidation durvalumab (category 1) for patients without progression after chemoradiation [14].

by_intent.patient_group: Extensive-stage SCLC (stage IV or T3–4 with multiple lung nodules)
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy alone is recommended; RT may be used for palliation of symptomatic sites (e.g., bone metastases, spinal cord compression, superior vena cava syndrome) [5,15]. First-line systemic therapy includes platinum/etoposide plus a PD-L1 inhibitor (atezolizumab or durvalumab, category 1) followed by maintenance immunotherapy [16,17]. Selected patients with low-bulk extrathoracic disease may receive consolidative thoracic RT after response to systemic therapy [18].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary evaluation is recommended before any surgery for SCLC [19]. Radiation oncology input, as part of a multidisciplinary evaluation or discussion, should be provided for all patients early in the determination of the treatment strategy [20]. The panel strongly recommends a second opinion with a pathologist specializing in thoracic malignancies for diagnostic dilemmas [21].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) guides treatment intensity. For LS-SCLC: PS 0–2 – concurrent chemoradiation (category 1). PS 3–4 due to SCLC – systemic therapy with or without RT (concurrent or sequential). PS 3–4 not due to SCLC – individualized treatment including supportive care [22]. For ES-SCLC: PS 0–2 – systemic therapy with or without RT to symptomatic sites. PS 3–4 due to SCLC – systemic therapy. PS 3–4 not due to SCLC – individualized therapy including supportive care [23]. For subsequent therapy: PS 0–2 may receive subsequent systemic therapy or palliative RT; PS 3–4 should receive palliative symptom management only [24]. Dose reduction or growth factor support should be considered for patients with PS 2 receiving subsequent therapy [25].

management_pathways.title: Limited Stage: Clinical Stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0) – Surgical Candidate
branching_basis: Clinical stage after standard evaluation (CT chest/upper abdomen, brain imaging, FDG-PET/CT, lymph node staging)
Pathologic lymph node staging (endobronchial ultrasound preferred, mediastinoscopy, etc.)

options.label: Lobectomy with mediastinal lymph node dissection or systematic sampling [10,26]
If R0 resection: systemic therapy alone (if N0) [27]
If R1/R2 resection: systemic therapy + concurrent RT [22]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Patients with N0 after complete resection receive systemic therapy alone. Those with R1/R2 receive chemoradiation.

options.label: Lobectomy with mediastinal lymph node dissection
Systemic therapy + mediastinal RT (concurrent or sequential) [28]
options.recommendation: Category 2A for N1, recommended for N2

adjuvant_logic.summary: For N+ disease, postoperative mediastinal RT is recommended (N2) or may be considered (N1) with systemic therapy [28].

management_pathways.title: Limited Stage: Clinical Stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0) – Medically Inoperable or Declines Surgery

nodes.criteria: Medically inoperable or decision made not to pursue surgical resection

options.label: Stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR) to primary tumor [12,13]
Systemic therapy (may be initiated first if time to SABR prolonged) [22]
Systemic therapy + concurrent RT (alternative to SABR) [22]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy is given after SABR; if systemic therapy is given first, it serves as induction.

options.label: Concurrent systemic therapy + RT (category 1) [8,9]

adjuvant_logic.summary: After completion of chemoradiation, response assessment then consolidation durvalumab for patients without progression (category 1) [14].

nodes.criteria: Poor performance status (PS 3–4) due to SCLC

options.label: Systemic therapy ± RT (concurrent or sequential) [22]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No consolidation immunotherapy recommended in this group.

nodes.criteria: Poor performance status (PS 3–4) not due to SCLC

options.label: Individualized treatment including supportive care [22]

management_pathways.title: Extensive Stage: Without Localized Symptomatic Sites or Brain Metastases

options.label: Systemic therapy including supportive care [23]

adjuvant_logic.summary: After 4 cycles (up to 6), continue maintenance immunotherapy if started with chemoimmunotherapy. Consider consolidative thoracic RT for selected patients with good response and low-bulk extrathoracic disease [18].

options.label: Systemic therapy [23]

nodes.criteria: Poor PS (3–4) not due to SCLC

options.label: Individualized therapy including supportive care [23]

management_pathways.title: Extensive Stage: With Localized Symptomatic Sites or Brain Metastases
branching_basis: Type of symptomatic site (SVC syndrome, bone metastases, spinal cord compression, brain metastases)
Symptom status (asymptomatic vs symptomatic brain metastases)

nodes.criteria: Superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome, lobar obstruction, or bone metastases (non-spinal cord compression)

options.label: Systemic therapy ± RT to symptomatic sites [23]
If high risk of fracture due to osseous structural impairment, consider orthopedic stabilization and palliative EBRT [23]

adjuvant_logic.summary: RT is palliative; continue systemic therapy as primary.

options.label: Initiate steroids (e.g., dexamethasone 10 mg load then 4–6 mg IV/PO every 4–6 hours) [23]
Systemic therapy + RT (typically sequential) [23]

adjuvant_logic.summary: RT is urgent; systemic therapy may be given sequentially.

options.label: Administer systemic therapy before initiating brain RT [23]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Brain RT given after completion of systemic therapy, or earlier if progression on therapy.

options.label: Initiate steroids for neurologic symptoms [23]
Brain RT before systemic therapy, unless immediate systemic therapy is indicated [23]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Brain RT is primary; systemic therapy given after or if RT cannot be started expeditiously.

management_pathways.title: Progressive Disease: Subsequent Therapy or Palliative Therapy

options.label: Subsequent systemic therapy (see preferred options: tarlatamab-dlle category 1, irinotecan, lurbinectedin, topotecan, re-treatment with platinum-based doublet if prolonged disease-free time, etc.) [24,29]
Palliative RT to symptomatic sites [24]
Palliative symptom management [24]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Continue systemic therapy until progression or unacceptable toxicity. For CNS progression only, continue systemic therapy and treat brain metastases with RT [24].

options.label: Palliative symptom management, including localized RT to symptomatic sites [24]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No subsequent systemic therapy generally recommended.

management_pathways.title: Transformed SCLC from NSCLC with an Oncogenic Driver
branching_basis: Histologic transformation from NSCLC to SCLC
Continued use of tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI)

options.label: Systemic cytotoxic chemotherapy using NCCN SCLC guidelines [30,31]
Role of immunotherapy is unclear; if TKI continued, ICI should be avoided due to known toxicity [31,32]
Consider referral to a center with expertise in managing transformed SCLC [31]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Limited data; treat per SCLC guidelines.

items.text: Complete history and physical examination, including assessment of signs and symptoms of SCLC (cough, hemoptysis, dyspnea, chest pain, SVC syndrome, neurologic symptoms, paraneoplastic syndromes) [7]
Smoking cessation counseling and intervention; see NCCN Guidelines for Smoking Cessation [33]

Pathologic evaluation to confirm histologic classification: SCLC is a poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma; distinguish from typical/atypical carcinoids and large-cell neuroendocrine carcinoma [34,35]. Key features: small blue cells, scant cytoplasm, high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, high mitotic count (>10 mitoses/2 mm²) [36]

Immunohistochemistry: nearly all SCLC positive for cytokeratin (AE1/AE3, CAM5.2); majority positive for neuroendocrine markers (INSM1, CD56, synaptophysin, chromogranin A); TTF-1 positive in 85–90%; Ki-67 typically 50–100% [37,38]. For suspicious morphology without neuroendocrine marker expression, consider POU2F3 IHC [39]

Biomarker testing via blood, tissue, or both can be considered in rare cases, particularly for ES-SCLC/relapsed patients who do not smoke, lightly smoke, have remote smoking history, or have diagnostic/therapeutic dilemma [40]
For diagnostic dilemma including carcinoid, strongly recommend second opinion from pathologist specializing in thoracic malignancies [21]
Electrolytes, liver function tests (LFTs), blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine [7]
Brain MRI with contrast (preferred) or CT with contrast if MRI contraindicated [42]
items.recommendation: Essential for all patients; more sensitive than CT for brain metastases

items.text: FDG-PET/CT scan (skull base to mid-thigh), if needed to clarify extent of disease [43]
items.recommendation: Recommended to clarify stage; if not available, bone scan may be used to identify bone metastases

items.text: Bone imaging (radiographs or MRI) as appropriate if FDG-PET/CT equivocal [44]
If pleural effusion present, thoracentesis recommended; if inconclusive, consider thoracoscopy [45]
Pulmonary function tests (PFTs) during evaluation for surgery or definitive RT [22]
Pathologic lymph node staging for stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0) to rule out occult nodal disease [46]. For stage IIB–IIIC, consider pathologic staging especially for cN0 to inform RT fields [22]
Unilateral bone marrow aspiration/biopsy in select patients (nucleated RBCs on peripheral smear, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia) only if it changes management [47]

Limited stage: Stage I–III (T any, N any, M0) that can be safely treated with definitive radiation doses. Excludes T3–4 due to multiple extensive lung nodules or tumor/nodal volume too large for tolerable radiation plan [48]
Extensive stage: Stage IV (T any, N any, M1a/b/c) or T3–4 due to multiple lung nodules too extensive or tumor/nodal volume too large for tolerable radiation plan [48]

surgery.role: Surgery is reserved for patients with clinical stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0) SCLC, diagnosed in <5% of SCLC patients [10]. The goal is curative resection followed by adjuvant systemic therapy. Patients most likely to benefit are those with SCLC clinical stage I–IIA after standard staging evaluation including CT chest/upper abdomen, brain imaging, FDG-PET/CT, and lymph node staging [10,49].
principles: Prior to resection, all patients should undergo mediastinoscopy or other surgical mediastinal staging to rule out occult nodal disease; this may also include endoscopic staging procedures [46].

For patients undergoing definitive surgical resection, the preferred operation is lobectomy with mediastinal lymph node dissection or systematic lymph node sampling (e.g., ≥3 N2 and ≥1 N1 stations) [10,26].
Pneumonectomy should not be performed if needed to encompass nodal metastatic disease; chemoradiation is the preferred alternative [10].

In patients who do not smoke, small lesions presumed to be SCLC on biopsy should be resected because they are likely carcinoids misdiagnosed (see NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors) [10].
Surgery may be considered for selected patients with T3 (based on size), N0 SCLC, if invasive mediastinal lymph node staging is negative [10].

For intraoperative diagnosis of likely SCLC without prior biopsy: mediastinal lymph node dissection or systematic sampling with frozen section is recommended; if primary site and lymph nodes appear resectable, perform anatomic resection (preferably lobectomy) [10].

procedures.name: Lobectomy
procedures.approach: Open or minimally invasive (video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery) with mediastinal lymph node dissection or systematic sampling
procedures.indications: Clinical stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0) after pathologic confirmation of N0 via mediastinal staging; also for selected T3,N0 based on size with negative invasive staging
procedures.key_complications: Hemorrhage, infection, prolonged air leak, arrhythmia, respiratory failure, injury to recurrent laryngeal nerve or phrenic nerve

procedures.name: Mediastinal lymph node dissection/sampling
procedures.approach: Systematic sampling of ≥3 N2 stations and ≥1 N1 station [26]
procedures.indications: Part of all surgical resections for SCLC; also as staging procedure when surgery is not planned (e.g., to inform RT fields)
procedures.key_complications: Bleeding, chyle leak, nerve injury, infection

procedures.name: Pneumonectomy (to be avoided if possible)
procedures.indications: Not recommended if needed to encompass nodal metastatic disease; chemoradiation preferred
procedures.key_complications: Higher morbidity and mortality compared to lobectomy; respiratory failure, bronchopleural fistula, empyema

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is defined by clinical stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0) after standard staging evaluation, including mediastinal staging to confirm N0. Patients with T3,N0 based on size may be considered for surgery if invasive mediastinal staging is negative. Patients with N1 or N2 disease are generally not surgical candidates; chemoradiation is recommended. Complete resection (R0) must be achievable; R1/R2 resection requires postoperative chemoradiation [10].

radiation_therapy.role: RT has a potential role in all stages of SCLC as part of either definitive or palliative therapy. Radiation oncology input should be provided early in the treatment strategy. Key components: appropriate simulation, accurate target definition, conformal RT planning, and accurate delivery. Minimum standard is CT-planned 3D-CRT; IMRT is preferred over 3D-CRT when concurrent chemotherapy is used due to reduced toxicity [20,50].
principles: RT concurrent with systemic therapy is standard and preferred to sequential chemoradiation for LS-SCLC [51]. RT should start early, with cycle 1 or 2 of systemic therapy (category 1) [52].
A shorter time from start of any therapy to the end of RT (SER) is significantly associated with improved survival [53].

Target definition based on pretreatment FDG-PET/CT and CT at RT planning, as well as positive biopsies; FDG-PET/CT recommended within 4 weeks and no more than 8 weeks before treatment, preferably in treatment position [20].

Historically, elective nodal irradiation (ENI) was included but modern series support omission of ENI with FDG-PET staging (isolated nodal recurrence rates <5%) [54,55]. Inclusion of ipsilateral hilum may be reasonable [20].
When using accelerated schedules (e.g., BID), allow ≥6-hour interfraction interval for normal tissue repair [56].

Normal tissue dose constraints: for similar RT prescription doses, constraints used for NSCLC are appropriate. For accelerated schedules, more conservative constraints should be used (e.g., spinal cord max ≤41 Gy for 45 Gy BID, ≤50 Gy for more protracted) [20].

dose_frameworks.name: Accelerated BID (standard)
dose_frameworks.indication: Limited-stage SCLC with concurrent chemotherapy; category 1 based on INT 0096 [56,57]

dose_frameworks.name: High-dose once-daily
dose_frameworks.indication: Limited-stage SCLC with concurrent chemotherapy; preferred if using once-daily fractionation [58,59]

dose_frameworks.name: High-dose accelerated (SIB)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 54 Gy (SIB to GTV) in 30 BID fractions over 3 weeks
dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: 1.5 Gy BID to PTV, SIB dose
dose_frameworks.indication: Limited-stage SCLC; phase 3 trial showed survival advantage over 45 Gy BID [60]

dose_frameworks.name: SABR for early-stage
dose_frameworks.total_dose: Varies (typically 30–34 Gy in 1 fraction, or 48–60 Gy in 3–5 fractions)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Daily or every other day over 1–2 weeks
dose_frameworks.indication: Clinical stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0) SCLC in medically inoperable patients or those declining surgery [12,13]

dose_frameworks.name: Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI)
dose_frameworks.indication: LS-SCLC with good response to initial therapy; also considered in ES-SCLC but with conflicting data. Preferred dose per PCI 99-01 [61,62]

dose_frameworks.name: Whole brain RT (WBRT) for brain metastases
dose_frameworks.indication: Brain metastases from SCLC; consider memantine during and after WBRT; hippocampal avoidance IMRT preferred for better prognosis [63,64]

dose_frameworks.name: Consolidative thoracic RT for ES-SCLC
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 30 Gy (in 10 fractions) up to definitive doses
dose_frameworks.schedule: Once daily over 2 weeks to several weeks
dose_frameworks.indication: Selected patients with ES-SCLC who have good response to systemic therapy, especially with residual thoracic disease and low-bulk extrathoracic metastases [18]

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT for extracranial metastases
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 30 Gy/10 fx, 20 Gy/5 fx, or 8 Gy/1 fx
dose_frameworks.indication: Palliation of symptomatic bone metastases, spinal cord compression, or other symptomatic sites [65]

approaches.name: Concurrent chemoradiation for LS-SCLC
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45 Gy in 30 BID fractions (category 1) or 66–70 Gy in 33–35 daily fractions
approaches.key_trial: INT 0096 (45 Gy BID vs 45 Gy QD, superior survival with BID) [56]; CONVERT (45 Gy BID vs 66 Gy QD, similar OS) [8]; CALGB 30610 (70 Gy QD vs 45 Gy BID, similar OS) [58]
approaches.toxicities: Esophagitis (grade 3–4 in ~9–19%), neutropenia (grade 4 in ~38–49%), pneumonitis, fatigue, cardiac toxicity

approaches.name: SABR followed by systemic therapy for early-stage SCLC
approaches.dose_fractionation: Various SABR regimens (e.g., 48–60 Gy in 3–5 fractions or 30–34 Gy in 1 fraction)
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: No concurrent; adjuvant systemic therapy after SABR [12,13]
approaches.indication: Clinical stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0) SCLC in medically inoperable or patients declining surgery
approaches.key_trial: Meta-analysis of SABR for stage I SCLC: 1-yr OS 86%, 2-yr OS 64% [66]; multicenter analysis showed improved OS with addition of chemotherapy (median 31.4 vs 14.3 months) [13]
approaches.toxicities: Grade 3 toxicity ~1.4% (pneumonitis, chest wall pain, rib fracture)

approaches.name: Consolidative thoracic RT for ES-SCLC
approaches.dose_fractionation: 30 Gy in 10 fractions up to definitive doses
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Given after systemic therapy; may be given during or before maintenance immunotherapy [18]
approaches.indication: Selected patients with ES-SCLC with complete or good response to systemic therapy, especially residual thoracic disease and low-bulk extrathoracic metastases
approaches.key_trial: CREST trial (30 Gy/10 fx): improved 2-yr OS (13% vs 3%) and 6-mo PFS; primary endpoint 1-yr OS not significantly improved [18]

approaches.name: PCI for LS-SCLC
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: No; administer after resolution of acute toxicities of initial therapy and before consolidation durvalumab if used [14]
approaches.indication: Patients with LS-SCLC who have good response to initial therapy; benefit unclear in pathologic stage I disease [61,62]
approaches.key_trial: Meta-analysis: PCI reduces 3-yr brain metastases from 58.6% to 33.3% and improves 3-yr OS from 15.3% to 20.7% [62]. RTOG 0212: neurotoxicity higher with higher dose; memantine may preserve cognitive function [63]
approaches.toxicities: Chronic neurotoxicity (cognitive decline, more common in age ≥60 years), fatigue, headache, nausea/vomiting. PCI not recommended for poor PS or impaired neurocognitive function.

approaches.name: WBRT for brain metastases
approaches.dose_fractionation: 30 Gy in 10 fractions; consider HA-IMRT plus memantine for better prognosis [64]
approaches.indication: Brain metastases from SCLC, especially multiple lesions or after PCI
approaches.key_trial: NRG CC001: HA-WBRT + memantine improved cognitive preservation vs conventional WBRT + memantine in patients with brain metastases (not SCLC-specific, but reasonable extrapolation) [64]. For limited brain metastases, SRS may be alternative (FIRE-SCLC cohort: SRS OS 6.5 mo vs WBRT 5.2 mo) [67]
approaches.toxicities: Cognitive decline, fatigue, alopecia, headache. Memantine reduces cognitive toxicity.

approaches.name: Palliative RT for symptoms
approaches.dose_fractionation: 30 Gy/10 fx, 20 Gy/5 fx, or 8 Gy/1 fx
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: No (but can be given with systemic therapy if needed)
approaches.indication: Symptomatic bone metastases, spinal cord compression, SVC syndrome, obstructive symptoms
approaches.key_trial: Standard palliative regimens for solid tumors; no SCLC-specific trials required
approaches.toxicities: Fatigue, local irritation, nausea/vomiting (if near GI tract)

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is essential at all stages of SCLC. For LS-SCLC, chemotherapy is given with concurrent RT (category 1), followed by durvalumab consolidation for those without progression (category 1) [8,14]. For ES-SCLC, first-line treatment is platinum/etoposide plus a PD-L1 inhibitor (atezolizumab or durvalumab, category 1) followed by maintenance immunotherapy [16,17]. Subsequent therapy for progressive disease includes tarlatamab-dlle (category 1), irinotecan, lurbinectedin, topotecan, platinum re-treatment, and others, depending on prior therapy and response [24]. For transformed SCLC from NSCLC, systemic cytotoxic chemotherapy per SCLC guidelines is recommended, with avoidance of ICIs if TKI continued [30,31]. Dose reduction or growth factor support should be considered for PS 2 patients [25].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cisplatin 75 mg/m² Day 1 + Etoposide 100 mg/m² Days 1–3 (category 2A, formerly category 1) [8]; or Cisplatin 60 mg/m² Day 1 + Etoposide 120 mg/m² Days 1–3 [56]; or Carboplatin AUC 5–6 Day 1 + Etoposide 100 mg/m² Days 1–3 [68]. Four cycles every 21–28 days during concurrent RT.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Cisplatin/etoposide is the most commonly used regimen based on superiority over alkylator/anthracycline regimens; carboplatin can be substituted for cisplatin [69].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cisplatin 25 mg/m² Days 1–3 + Etoposide 100 mg/m² Days 1–3 [70]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Limited-stage SCLC: consolidation after chemoradiation (no progression)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Durvalumab 1500 mg Day 1 every 28 days for up to 24 months (category 1) [14]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ADRIATIC trial: median OS 55.9 months vs 33.4 months (HR 0.73, P=0.01); median PFS 16.6 vs 9.2 months (HR 0.76) [14].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: None; not recommended if progression before consolidation.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Carboplatin AUC 5 Day 1 + Etoposide 100 mg/m² Days 1–3 + Atezolizumab 1200 mg Day 1 every 21 days x 4 cycles, followed by maintenance atezolizumab (1200 mg every 21 days or 1680 mg every 28 days) (category 1) [16,71]; or Carboplatin AUC 5–6 Day 1 + Etoposide 80–100 mg/m² Days 1–3 + Durvalumab 1500 mg Day 1 every 21 days x 4 cycles, followed by maintenance durvalumab 1500 mg every 28 days (category 1) [17]; or Cisplatin 75–80 mg/m² Day 1 + Etoposide 80–100 mg/m² Days 1–3 + Durvalumab (same schedule) [17]. Also: Carboplatin/Etoposide + Atezolizumab induction then maintenance lurbinectedin 3.2 mg/m² + atezolizumab for patients with at least stable disease, ECOG PS 0–1, no history of brain metastases (category 1) [72].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: IMpower133: atezolizumab regimen improved OS (12.3 vs 10.3 months, HR 0.76) [16]. CASPIAN: durvalumab regimen improved OS (13.0 vs 10.3 months, HR 0.73) [17]. IMforte: lurbinectedin/atezolizumab maintenance improved PFS and OS in selected patients [72].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Carboplatin AUC 5–6 + Etoposide 100 mg/m² (without ICI) [73]; Cisplatin 75 mg/m² Day 1 + Etoposide 100 mg/m² Days 1–3 [74]; Cisplatin 80 mg/m² Day 1 + Etoposide 80 mg/m² Days 1–3 [75]; Cisplatin 25 mg/m² Days 1–3 + Etoposide 100 mg/m² Days 1–3 [76]; Irinotecan-based regimens (useful in certain circumstances): Carboplatin AUC 5 Day 1 + Irinotecan 50 mg/m² Days 1,8,15 [77]; Cisplatin 60 mg/m² Day 1 + Irinotecan 60 mg/m² Days 1,8,15 [78]; Cisplatin 30 mg/m² Days 1,8 + Irinotecan 65 mg/m² Days 1,8 [79].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Extensive-stage SCLC: maintenance after first-line chemoimmunotherapy (if no progression)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Continue atezolizumab 1200 mg every 21 days or 1680 mg every 28 days, or durvalumab 1500 mg every 28 days, until progression or intolerable toxicity [16,17]. For those started on atezolizumab and meeting criteria: lurbinectedin 3.2 mg/m² + atezolizumab 1200 mg Day 1 every 21 days [72].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Maintenance immunotherapy improves outcomes over chemotherapy alone; lurbinectedin added benefit in selected patients [72].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Relapsed or progressive SCLC: subsequent therapy (PS 0–2)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Tarlatamab-dlle (category 1) [29]; Irinotecan [80]; Lurbinectedin (if not previously used) [81]; re-treatment with platinum-based doublet with or without immunotherapy if prolonged disease-free time [82]; Topotecan (oral or IV) [83,84].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Tarlatamab phase 3: median OS 13.6 vs 8.3 months vs chemotherapy [29]. Lurbinectedin basket trial: overall response rate 35% [81]. Topotecan vs CAV: similar response rates, less myelosuppression [83].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: CAV [83]; Docetaxel [85]; Gemcitabine [86]; Nivolumab or pembrolizumab (if no prior ICI) [87,88]; Oral etoposide [89]; Paclitaxel [90]; Temozolomide [91].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Transformed SCLC from NSCLC with oncogenic driver
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Systemic cytotoxic chemotherapy per SCLC guidelines (platinum/etoposide) [30,31].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Very limited data; best evidence supports SCLC-type chemotherapy [30].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Referral to center with expertise; avoid ICI if TKI continued [31].

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Etoposide (LS-SCLC, concurrent RT)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nephrotoxicity, neuropathy, ototoxicity, nausea/vomiting, myelosuppression, fatigue

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Etoposide (LS-SCLC, concurrent RT)
key_regimens.setting: Limited-stage SCLC primary chemoradiation; also alternative for ES-SCLC

key_regimens.name: Durvalumab (consolidation, LS-SCLC)
key_regimens.setting: After chemoradiation for LS-SCLC without progression
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events (pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, dermatitis, endocrinopathies), fatigue, infusion reactions

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Etoposide + Atezolizumab (ES-SCLC first-line)
key_regimens.cycle_days: 21 days x 4 cycles, then maintenance atezolizumab 1200 mg every 21 days or 1680 mg every 28 days
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, nausea, fatigue, immune-related adverse events (pneumonitis, hepatitis, colitis, endocrinopathies), infusion reactions

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Etoposide + Durvalumab (ES-SCLC first-line)
key_regimens.cycle_days: 21 days x 4 cycles, then maintenance durvalumab 1500 mg every 28 days
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Same as above; also includes patients with asymptomatic untreated brain metastases [17]

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Etoposide + Atezolizumab → Lurbinectedin/Atezolizumab (ES-SCLC maintenance)
key_regimens.setting: Extensive-stage SCLC first-line induction then maintenance for patients with at least stable disease, ECOG PS 0–1, no brain metastases
key_regimens.cycle_days: Induction: 21 days x 4 cycles; Maintenance: 21 days until progression
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia, fatigue, increased LFTs, immune-related adverse events. Lurbinectedin adds myelosuppression and hepatotoxicity.

key_regimens.name: Tarlatamab-dlle (subsequent therapy)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Cytokine release syndrome (56%), neutropenia, lymphopenia, fatigue, hyponatremia, anemia. Manage per NCCN Guidelines for Management of CAR T-Cell and Lymphocyte Engager-Related Toxicities [29].

key_regimens.name: Irinotecan (subsequent therapy)
key_regimens.setting: Relapsed SCLC; consider for CNS disease

drugs.name: Irinotecan
drugs.dose: 300–350 mg/m² (or 125 mg/m² weekly for 4 doses every 6 weeks)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Diarrhea (early and delayed), myelosuppression, nausea, vomiting, alopecia, pulmonary toxicity

key_regimens.name: Topotecan (subsequent therapy)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia), fatigue, nausea, alopecia

treatment_response_assessment.title: Response Assessment and Surveillance
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment timing depends on stage and treatment modality. For LS-SCLC receiving adjuvant systemic therapy ± RT, assessment is recommended only after completion of adjuvant therapy. For LS-SCLC receiving systemic therapy + concurrent RT, assessment after completion of initial therapy. Repeating scans during adjuvant or initial treatment is not recommended in the absence of new symptoms [92]. For systemic therapy alone or sequential therapy, assessment after every 2 cycles of systemic therapy and at completion [92]. For ES-SCLC during systemic therapy, assessment after every 2–3 cycles and at completion. For known brain metastases, brain MRI every 3–4 months or per clinical indication; if systemic therapy given before brain RT, brain imaging repeated every 2 cycles until RT [92].
response_logic: For LS-SCLC: after completion of primary treatment, if complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) or stable disease (SD): proceed to surveillance for limited stage. For good PS, consider durvalumab consolidation (category 1) and PCI or MRI brain surveillance. For poor PS, durvalumab alone without PCI recommended. For extensive stage with CR/PR/SD: continue maintenance immunotherapy if started on chemoimmunotherapy; consider MRI brain surveillance ± PCI; consider thoracic RT for selected patients [92].
For primary progressive disease (PPD): proceed to subsequent therapy/palliative therapy pathway [24].
For subsequent therapy: continue until progression or unacceptable toxicity. For CNS progression only, continue systemic therapy and treat brain metastases with RT [24].
imaging_recommendations: Response assessment imaging: For limited stage, C/A/P CT with contrast and brain MRI (preferred) or brain CT with contrast after completion of adjuvant therapy or initial therapy [92].
For extensive stage, C/A/P CT with contrast after every 2–3 cycles of systemic therapy and at completion. Brain MRI or CT with contrast every 3–4 months for known brain metastases [92].

Surveillance after completion of initial therapy: For limited stage, oncology follow-up visits every 3 months during years 1–2, every 6 months during year 3, then annually. For extensive stage, every 2 months during year 1, every 3–4 months during years 2–3, every 6 months during years 4–5, then annually [92].

Surveillance imaging: CT chest ± abdomen/pelvis every 2–6 months (more frequently in years 1–2, less frequently thereafter). Brain MRI or CT with contrast every 3–4 months during year 1, then every 6 months in year 2, then as clinically indicated, regardless of PCI status [92].
New pulmonary nodule should initiate workup for potential new primary lung cancer [92].
FDG-PET/CT is not recommended for routine follow-up unless contrast CT or MRI is contraindicated [92].
Blood work (CBC, electrolytes, LFTs, BUN, creatinine) as clinically indicated [92].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: For progressive disease, consider biomarker testing if not previously done to determine clinical trial eligibility [24].
For CNS progression only, continue systemic therapy and treat brain metastases with RT; do not discontinue systemic therapy unless extracranial progression also [24].

Salvage therapy options: subsequent systemic therapy as per algorithm for PS 0–2; palliative symptom management for PS 3–4. Consider re-treatment with platinum-based regimen if prolonged disease-free time. Tarlatamab-dlle is a category 1 preferred option for subsequent therapy after platinum-based chemotherapy [24,29].
For transformed SCLC from NSCLC, consider referral to a center with expertise; use SCLC-type chemotherapy; avoid ICIs if TKI is continued [31].

prognosis.overall: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) is characterized by rapid doubling time, high growth fraction, and early development of widespread metastases. Although highly sensitive to initial chemotherapy and radiation, most patients eventually die of recurrent disease [17]. Long-term survival in extensive-stage disease is rare [16]. Age <70 years, normal lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and stage I disease are associated with favorable prognosis in limited-stage SCLC (LS-SCLC). Younger age, good performance status (PS), normal creatinine, normal LDH, and a single metastatic site are favorable prognostic factors in extensive-stage SCLC (ES-SCLC). Poor PS (3–4), extensive-stage disease, weight loss, and elevated LDH are the most important adverse prognostic factors [82,83].

by_stage.context: For patients receiving cisplatin/etoposide plus definitive thoracic radiotherapy, reported 5-year overall survival (OS) rates are 31% to 34%, with median OS of 25 to 30 months and response rates of 70% to 90% [105]. In the ADRIATIC trial, patients with LS-SCLC who received consolidation durvalumab after chemoradiation had a median OS of 55.9 months vs. 33.4 months with placebo [115].

by_stage.stage: Extensive-stage SCLC (stage IV or T3-4 not safely encompassed in a radiation plan)
by_stage.context: Long-term survival is rare [16]. In the IMpower133 trial, 1-year OS was 51.9% in the atezolizumab arm vs. 39.0% with chemotherapy alone; median OS was 12.3 vs. 10.3 months (HR 0.76; 95% CI 0.6–0.95; p=0.0154) [118,120]. In the CASPIAN trial, 1-year OS was 52.8% in the durvalumab arm vs. 39.3% with chemotherapy alone; median OS 13.0 vs. 10.3 months (HR 0.73; 95% CI 0.59–0.91; p=0.0047) [119,123,124]. Survival is improved with chemoimmunotherapy compared to chemotherapy alone.

prognostic_factors: Limited-stage: Age <70 years, normal LDH, stage I disease are favorable [82,83].
Extensive-stage: Younger age, good PS, normal creatinine, normal LDH, single metastatic site are favorable [82,83].
Adverse: Poor PS (3–4), extensive stage, weight loss, elevated LDH [82,83].
prognosis.prognostic_context: SCLC has a very high initial response to therapy, but nearly all patients eventually relapse. The aggressive biology necessitates expedited workup and treatment initiation. Prognosis has improved modestly with the incorporation of immunotherapy, but remains poor overall, especially for extensive-stage disease.

disease_related.complication: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion (SIADH)
disease_related.management: Fluid restriction, saline infusion for symptomatic patients, demeclocycline, vasopressin receptor inhibitors (conivaptan, tolvaptan) for refractory hyponatremia [SCL-D].
Consider ketoconazole; if not effective, consider metyrapone. Referral to endocrinology subspecialist [SCL-D].
Consider amifampridine or intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) in consultation with neurology [SCL-D].
Consider early subspecialty consultation; comprehensive paraneoplastic antibody panel if suspected [SCL-A, MS-4].

disease_related.complication: Superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome, spinal cord compression
disease_related.management: Systemic therapy ± RT to symptomatic sites; for spinal cord compression, initiate steroids (e.g., dexamethasone 10 mg loading dose followed by 4–6 mg every 4–6 hours) and systemic therapy + RT (typically sequential) [SCL-5].
Brain RT (WBRT or SRS) with memantine; for asymptomatic patients, systemic therapy may precede brain RT [SCL-5, SCL-F 4 of 7].

chemotherapy: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia) – can be ameliorated with growth factors or trilaciclib in extensive-stage [SCL-D, SCL-E 2 of 6].
Nausea/vomiting – managed with antiemetics per NCCN Antiemesis Guidelines [SCL-D].
Fatigue, alopecia, cytopenias common; cisplatin carries risk of nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, ototoxicity; carboplatin has greater myelosuppression [MS-9].
Irinotecan may cause diarrhea and pulmonary toxicity [MS-16].
immunotherapy: Immune-mediated adverse events (e.g., pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies) – high-dose corticosteroids generally recommended; ICIs should be withheld or discontinued for severe/life-threatening events [MS-10, MS-19].
Cytokine release syndrome with tarlatamab – observed in 56% of patients [MS-16]; manage per NCCN Guidelines for Management of CAR T-Cell and Lymphocyte Engager-Related Toxicities [SCL-E 3 of 6].
targeted_therapy: Limited data in SCLC; for transformed SCLC from NSCLC with oncogenic driver, TKI continued with ICI avoided due to known toxicity [SCL-E 4 of 6].
surgery: Standard resection is lobectomy with mediastinal lymph node dissection; pneumonectomy is not recommended if required to encompass nodal metastatic disease [SCL-C].
Postoperative systemic therapy recommended; mediastinal RT for N+ disease [SCL-C].
radiation: Esophagitis (grade 3-4 in ~9% with concurrent chemoradiation), pneumonitis (especially with accelerated schedules), neurocognitive decline with PCI (especially in patients >60 years) [MS-20, MS-21, MS-25].
Spinal cord dose constraints must be observed; for accelerated schedules, max cord dose ≤41 Gy [SCL-F 3 of 7].
Consolidative thoracic RT in extensive-stage may cause chest wall toxicity but is well-tolerated [MS-23].

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is integral from diagnosis. The NCCN Guidelines recommend early integration of palliative care to address cancer-related symptoms and goals of care [SCL-1]. All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Not explicitly discussed in the SCLC guideline; however, the NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care may provide guidance. Smoking cessation, including counseling and pharmacotherapy, is strongly promoted [SCL-D].
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Refer to the NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis [SCL-D]. Chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting is managed according to standard antiemetic protocols.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF) or granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) are not recommended during concurrent systemic therapy plus RT (category 1 for not using GM-CSF) [1,2]. Trilaciclib or G-CSF may be used as prophylactic options to decrease chemotherapy-induced myelosuppression when administering platinum/etoposide ± ICI regimens or topotecan for extensive-stage SCLC [SCL-D].
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Not specifically addressed in the SCLC guideline; the NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care may provide recommendations.
supportive_care.pain_management: Refer to the NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain [SCL-D].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Refer to the NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management; use the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health [SCL-D].
supportive_care.dental_care: Not specifically addressed; however, general oncology dental care principles apply.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After completion of initial therapy for limited-stage SCLC, oncology follow-up visits are recommended every 3 months during years 1–2, every 6 months during year 3, then annually. For extensive-stage SCLC, visits every 2 months during year 1, every 3–4 months during years 2–3, every 6 months during years 4–5, then annually [SCL-7]. A survivorship care plan should be provided after completion of initial therapy [SCL-7].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Most patients relapse within 2 years; surveillance aims to detect recurrence early, manage brain metastases before debilitating neurologic symptoms, and identify second primary lung cancers. The risk of recurrence declines over time [189]. Smoking cessation reduces the risk of second primary tumors [192-194].
late_effects_screening: Neurocognitive decline after PCI – assess in patients >60 years; consider memantine and hippocampal avoidance [SCL-F 3-4 of 7].
Second primary lung cancers – new pulmonary nodule should initiate workup for potential new primary [SCL-7].
Chronic effects of chemotherapy (e.g., neuropathy, renal impairment) and radiation (e.g., pulmonary fibrosis, cardiac toxicity).
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Most SCLCs relapse within 2 years. Brain metastases are common; approximately 10-15% present with CNS metastases at diagnosis, and >50% develop intracranial metastases over time [MS-6, MS-24]. Chest recurrences are frequent if residual thoracic disease. Disease progression after initial response is nearly universal [17].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Limited-stage: Oncology visits every 3 months (y 1-2), every 6 months (y 3), then annually [SCL-7].
Extensive-stage: Every 2 months (y 1), every 3-4 months (y 2-3), every 6 months (y 4-5), then annually [SCL-7].
History and physical; blood work only as clinically indicated [SCL-7].
imaging_strategy: CT chest ± abdomen/pelvis every 2-6 months (more frequently in years 1-2, less frequently thereafter) [SCL-G 2 of 3].
Brain MRI (preferred) or CT with contrast every 3-4 months during year 1, then every 6 months in year 2, then as clinically indicated (regardless of PCI status) [SCL-7, SCL-G 2 of 3].
For patients with known metastases, imaging of those sites should be repeated as clinically indicated [SCL-G 2 of 3].
FDG-PET/CT is not recommended for routine follow-up unless contrast CT or MRI is contraindicated [SCL-G 2 of 3].
laboratory_monitoring: CBC, electrolytes, LFTs, BUN, creatinine may be obtained as clinically indicated during surveillance [SCL-6].
No specific routine laboratory schedule is recommended; monitoring is based on clinical judgment.
supportive_follow_up: Smoking cessation intervention (see NCCN Guidelines for Smoking Cessation) [SCL-7].
Survivorship care plan per NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship [SCL-7].
Consider psychosocial support and distress screening [SCL-D].

special_situations.situation: Transformed SCLC from NSCLC with an oncogenic driver
special_situations.key_message: This is a rare population with very limited data to guide treatment. Systemic cytotoxic chemotherapy using the NCCN Guidelines for Small Cell Lung Cancer is recommended. The role of immunotherapy is unclear based on limited data. If a tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) is continued, immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) should be avoided due to known toxicity. Consider referral to a center with experience managing transformed SCLC [SCL-E 4 of 6].
special_situations.management: Systemic cytotoxic chemotherapy per SCLC regimens (e.g., platinum/etoposide). Avoid ICI if TKI continued [43-48]. Consider referral to an expert center.
special_situations.evidence: Limited to retrospective series and case reports. References [43-48] from SCL-E 6 of 6.

special_situations.situation: Poor performance status (PS 3-4) due to SCLC
special_situations.key_message: Patients with PS 3-4 due to SCLC may still benefit from systemic therapy ± RT, but those with PS 3-4 not due to SCLC should receive individualized treatment including supportive care [SCL-4, SCL-5]. PCI is not recommended in patients with poor PS or impaired neurocognitive function [SCL-6A].
special_situations.management: For limited-stage: systemic therapy ± RT (concurrent or sequential). For extensive-stage: individualized therapy including supportive care [SCL-4, SCL-5]. See NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care.

special_situations.key_message: Brain MRI is preferred for detection. For asymptomatic brain metastases, administer systemic therapy before brain RT. For symptomatic brain metastases, initiate steroids and brain RT before systemic therapy unless immediate systemic therapy is indicated [SCL-5]. If brain metastases progress while on systemic therapy, brain RT should be initiated before completion of systemic therapy [SCL-5].
special_situations.management: Steroid (e.g., dexamethasone) for symptomatic neurologic disease. Brain RT (WBRT 30 Gy in 10 fractions, SRS, or HA-WBRT with memantine). Systemic therapy as indicated [SCL-5, SCL-F 4 of 7].

clinical_pearls: SCLC is defined by rapid doubling time and early dissemination; workup must be expedited with studies performed in parallel [SCL-1].
For limited-stage SCLC, concurrent chemoradiation is standard (category 1 for PS 0-2); RT should start with cycle 1 or 2 [SCL-4, SCL-F 1 of 7].
Accelerated RT (45 Gy BID over 3 weeks) is category 1; once-daily 66-70 Gy is also acceptable, but higher-dose accelerated RT may improve survival [SCL-F 1-2 of 7, MS-21].
For extensive-stage SCLC, chemoimmunotherapy (atezolizumab or durvalumab with platinum/etoposide) is the preferred first-line approach (category 1) [SCL-E 1 of 6, MS-11].
Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) reduces brain metastases but increases neurocognitive toxicity, especially in patients >60 years; MRI surveillance is an alternative [SCL-F 3-4 of 7, MS-24].
Surgery is reserved for clinical stage I-IIA (T1-2,N0,M0) after thorough pathologic nodal staging; less than 5% of patients are eligible [SCL-C].
Subsequent therapy options include tarlatamab (category 1), clinical trials, irinotecan, lurbinectedin, re-treatment with platinum doublet, and topotecan [SCL-E 3 of 6].

Biomarker testing (blood, tissue, or both) may be considered in rare cases, particularly for extensive-stage/relapsed SCLC in never/light smokers or those with a diagnostic dilemma [SCL-1, SCL-B 1 of 2].

key_trials.full_name: A Phase III Trial of Atezolizumab Plus Carboplatin and Etoposide in Extensive-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer
key_trials.intervention: Carboplatin/etoposide + atezolizumab followed by maintenance atezolizumab
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 12.3 months (atezolizumab) vs. 10.3 months (placebo); HR 0.76 (95% CI 0.6–0.95; p=0.0154). 1-year OS 51.7% vs. 38.2% (updated 2021: 51.9% vs. 39.0%) [118,120].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PFS HR 0.77 (95% CI 0.63–0.95; p=0.0155); response rate 60% vs. 64%; grade 3-4 adverse events 67.7% vs. 63.3%.
key_trials.practice_change: Established chemoimmunotherapy as first-line standard for extensive-stage SCLC.

key_trials.full_name: Durvalumab plus platinum-etoposide versus platinum-etoposide alone in first-line treatment of extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (CASPIAN)
key_trials.intervention: Durvalumab + platinum (carboplatin or cisplatin)/etoposide followed by maintenance durvalumab
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 13.0 months (durvalumab) vs. 10.3 months (control); HR 0.73 (95% CI 0.59–0.91; p=0.0047). 3-year OS 17.6% vs. 5.8% [119,123,124].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Progression-free survival HR 0.78 (95% CI 0.65–0.94); response rate 68% vs. 58%. Serious adverse events 32% vs. 36%.
key_trials.practice_change: Provided an alternative chemoimmunotherapy option (durvalumab) for ES-SCLC; included patients with asymptomatic brain metastases.

key_trials.full_name: Durvalumab after chemoradiotherapy in limited-stage small-cell lung cancer (ADRIATIC)
key_trials.population: Limited-stage SCLC, stage I-III, PS 0-1, no progression after chemoradiation (N=530 randomized to durvalumab vs. placebo; also had durvalumab/tremelimumab arm)
key_trials.intervention: Durvalumab consolidation for up to 24 months (1500 mg every 4 weeks)
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 55.9 months (durvalumab) vs. 33.4 months (placebo) (HR 0.73; p=0.01). Median PFS 16.6 vs. 9.2 months (HR 0.68; p=0.02) [115].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS at 24 months: 68.0% vs. 58.5%; PFS at 18 months: 48.8% vs. 36.1%. Grade 3-4 adverse events 30% vs. 31%.
key_trials.practice_change: Established durvalumab consolidation as a new standard after chemoradiation for LS-SCLC (category 1).

key_trials.full_name: Efficacy and safety of first-line maintenance therapy with lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab in extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (IMforte)
key_trials.n: Not explicitly stated in excerpt; phase 3 randomized trial comparing lurbinectedin + atezolizumab vs. atezolizumab alone after induction chemoimmunotherapy.
key_trials.population: Extensive-stage SCLC with at least stable disease after 4 cycles of carboplatin/etoposide + atezolizumab; ECOG PS 0-1; no history of brain metastases
key_trials.intervention: Lurbinectedin 3.2 mg/m2 + atezolizumab 1200 mg every 21 days as maintenance
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Potential for increased toxicity with combination; <10% of control arm received lurbinectedin after progression.
key_trials.practice_change: Addition of lurbinectedin to maintenance atezolizumab is a category 1 option for selected ES-SCLC patients.

key_trials.full_name: Tarlatamab in small-cell lung cancer after platinum-based chemotherapy
key_trials.population: Relapsed/refractory SCLC after platinum-based chemotherapy (N=254 tarlatamab, N=255 chemotherapy)
key_trials.intervention: Tarlatamab-dlle 10 mg IV (bispecific T-cell engager targeting DLL3 and CD3)
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 13.6 months (tarlatamab) vs. 8.3 months (chemotherapy); 6-month OS 76% vs. 62%; 12-month OS 53% vs. 37%. Grade ≥3 adverse events 54% vs. 80% [196, 16].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Cytokine release syndrome in 56% of tarlatamab patients (mostly low-grade).
key_trials.practice_change: Tarlatamab is a category 1, preferred subsequent therapy for SCLC with disease progression on or after platinum-based chemotherapy.
